analogy: Think of "Power Sharing" through the chapter's own opening images: P o w e r - s h a r i n g 1 Chapter I Power-sharing Overview With this chapter, we resume the tour of democracy that we started last year. We noted last year that in a democracy all power does not rest... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Power Sharing is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that p o w e r - s h a r i n g 1 Chapter I Power-sharing Overview With this chapter, we resume the tour of democracy that we started last year.

The text explains that we noted last year that in a democracy all power does not rest with any one organ of the government.

The text explains that an intelligent sharing of power among legislature, executive and judiciary is very important to the design of a democracy.

The text explains that in this and the next two chapters, we carry this idea of power-sharing forward.

The text explains that we start with two stories from Belgium and Sri Lanka.

The text explains that both these stories are about how democracies handle demands for power-sharing.

The text explains that the stories yield some general conclusions about the need for power-sharing in democracy.

The text explains that this allows us to discuss various forms of power-sharing that will be taken up in the following two chapters.

The text explains that 2 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s Belgium and Sri Lanka I have a simple equation in mind.

The text explains that sharing power = dividing power = weakening the country.

The text explains that ethnic: A social division based on shared culture.

The text explains that people belonging to the same ethnic group believe in their common descent because of similarities of physical type or of culture or both.

The text explains that they need not always have the same religion or nationality.

The text explains that belgium is a small country in Europe, smaller in area than the state of Haryana.

The text explains that it has borders with France, the Netherlands, Germany and Luxembourg.

The text explains that it has a population of a little over one crore, about half the population of Haryana.

The text explains that you will notice that place names and directions in two languages – French and Dutch. © Wikipedia

The text explains that one basic principle of democracy is that people are the source of all political power.

The text explains that 4 Power sharing arrangements can also be seen in the way political parties, pressure groups and movements control or influence those in power.

The text explains that sometimes this kind of sharing can be direct, when two or more parties form an alliance to contest elections.

The text explains that they propose to have a common list of candidates in the next parliamentary elections.  The finance ministers of various states in Nigeria got together and demanded that the federal government declare its sources of income.

The text explains that different arguments are usually put forth in favour of and against power sharing.

The text explains that identify those which are in favour of power sharing and select the answer using the codes given below?

The text explains that power sharing: A. reduces conflict among different communities B. decreases the possibility of arbitrariness C. delays decision making process D. accommodates diversities E. increases instability and divisiveness F. promotes people’s participation in government G. undermines the unity of a country Exercises (a) A B D F (b) A C E F (c) A B D G (d) B C D G

The text explains that consider the following two statements on power sharing and select the answer using the codes given below: A.

The text explains that it helps to reduce the possibility of conflict between social groups.

The text explains that which of these statements are true and false?

The text explains that (a) A is true but B is false (b) Both A and B are true (c) Both A and B are false (d) A is false but B is true List I List II 1.

The text explains that power shared among different organs of government A.

The text explains that power shared among governments at different levels B.

The text explains that power shared by two or more political parties D.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II, Chapter 1: Power Sharing

schemes.name: We
schemes.point: We noted last year that in a democracy all power does not rest with any one organ of the government. (NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II Ch1 pg 1)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The leaders of the Sinhala community sought to secure dominance over government by virtue of their majority. (NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II Ch1 pg 2)

schemes.name: As
schemes.point: As a result, the democratically elected government adopted a series of majoritarian measures to establish Sinhala supremacy. (NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II Ch1 pg 2)

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In 1956, an Act was passed to recognise Sinhala as the only official language, thus disregarding Tamil. (NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II Ch1 pg 2)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The governments followed preferential policies that favoured Sinhala applicants for university positions and government jobs. (NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II Ch1 pg 2)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1 Power is shared among different and 2 Power can be shared among as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Power Sharing; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Power Sharing through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1 Power is shared among different and 2 Power can be shared among to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
